mnemonics.title: COPiE - Class Components
mnemonics.content: C-O-P-i-E stands for: Class = Objects + Properties + Inheritance (later) + Encapsulation - A class contains data (attributes) and behavior (methods) bundled together, like a blueprint that defines what an object will have and do.

mnemonics.title: S.E.L.F. - Remember 'self'
mnemonics.content: S-E-L-F: S tands for S ame instance, E very L oop uses F unction - 'self' always refers to the current instance of the class. It's like 'I' when you talk about yourself - every method uses self to access or modify the object's own attributes.

mnemonics.title: Dunder Init: 'I' Build It
mnemonics.content: __init__ = 'I' INITIALIZE - This is the constructor that 'initializes' or sets up the new object with starting values. Think of it as the 'birth certificate' that fills in the object's initial details when it's created.

mnemonics.title: BluePrint = BP
mnemonics.content: BP = BluePrint - Just as an architect's blueprint defines what a building will have (rooms, windows, doors), a Python Class defines what objects of that type will have (attributes) and can do (methods).

acronyms.usage: The programming paradigm used in Python where we organize code into 'objects' that contain data (attributes) and functions (methods). Remember: Everything in Python is an object!
Special method automatically called when an object is created. Use it to set initial values for attributes. Called automatically: obj = ClassName() triggers __init__ internally.
Dunder method that returns a human-readable string when print(obj) is called. Use for debugging and display purposes.
First parameter in every instance method. It refers to the current object instance. All instance attributes must be accessed via self.attribute_name.

visualTips.visualization: Picture an architectural blueprint hanging on a wall. The blueprint itself doesn't have real rooms you can enter - it's just a plan. Now picture multiple houses built FROM that blueprint - those are the objects. Each house has actual rooms (attributes) and can perform actions (methods like 'open_door()').

Think of a cookie cutter (Class) pressed into dough. Each cookie that comes out is an Object. All cookies look the same initially (same shape/attributes from the class), but you can add unique decorations (change individual attribute values) to each cookie.

Visualize a Student class as a file folder with labeled sections: Name, Roll No, Marks - each section holds specific data. These labeled sections are the 'attributes' - variables stored inside the object.

Picture a remote control (Class) with buttons. Each button triggers an action on your TV. The remote's buttons are like 'methods' - functions that perform actions. Pressing 'power button' is like calling obj.power_on().

analogies.analogy: Class = The concept of a 'Vehicle' - a general category. Object = Your specific car 'Swift Dzire' with license plate DL-01-CS-1234. The idea of a vehicle doesn't go anywhere, but your specific car does.
Think of a Person class like a government ID card. The card has fields: Name, DOB, Address - these are the 'attributes' or data stored. Every person's ID card holds different values for these fields.

A BankAccount class is like a bank passbook. The passbook has data (balance, account number) and operations (deposit(), withdraw(), check_balance()). You perform actions through the passbook, just like calling methods on an object.

When you say 'I want to eat', 'I' refers to yourself - the current person speaking. In Python, 'self' is that 'I' - it refers to the specific object currently performing the action. Each object has its own 'I'.

When you buy a new smartphone, it comes with factory settings - your name isn't there yet, contacts empty. The __init__ method is like the 'setup wizard' when you first turn on the phone, where you enter your name, set language, configure WiFi - initializing all starting values.
